content.text: When a language model produces text, the computation that converts raw numeric scores over a vocabulary into an actual sequence of words is called

. The choice of decoding method fundamentally shapes output quality, diversity, speed, and determinism — making it one of the highest-leverage knobs in any LLM-powered system. Greedy decoding, beam search, temperature sampling, top-k filtering, and nucleus (top-p) sampling are the primary strategies, each encoding a different trade-off between optimality, efficiency, and creativity.

A language model learns a conditional probability distribution over the vocabulary at each position in a sequence. Given a prompt (input context) and all tokens generated so far, the model outputs a vector of
— one real number per vocabulary token. After passing these logits through a softmax function, you get a probability distribution
over the next token. Decoding is the algorithm that uses this distribution to select the actual next token, repeating the process until a stopping criterion is reached.
Decoding methods fall into two broad families.
— greedy decoding and beam search — always produce the same output for the same input.

— temperature scaling, top-k, and top-p/nucleus sampling — introduce randomness so that the same input yields different outputs on different runs. Both families have well-defined use cases, and many production systems combine them (e.g., beam search with length penalties, or top-p with temperature).

operation — it does not change the model weights or the probability distributions the model computes. It only decides which tokens to pick from those distributions at each step. This means the same model checkpoint can produce radically different outputs depending solely on the decoding configuration.
The objective of autoregressive text generation is to produce the most probable

, not merely the most probable next token. These two objectives are different — and this gap is the central tension in decoding. Greedy decoding solves the simpler per-token objective and is extremely fast, but it can lock the model into suboptimal paths early. Beam search approximates the harder sequence-level objective and typically improves quality, at the cost of k× inference time per step. Sampling methods sacrifice optimality for diversity and creativity, which matter enormously for tasks like story generation, brainstorming, and dialogue.

In agentic AI systems, decoding choices have compound effects. An agent that generates intermediate reasoning steps (chain-of-thought), selects tool calls, or drafts multi-step plans relies on decoding at every turn. A poorly chosen strategy can cause the agent to commit to wrong reasoning early (greedy), produce repetitive outputs (low-temperature beam search), or hallucinate wildly (high-temperature sampling). Understanding which strategy to use — and how to tune it — is foundational to building reliable AI pipelines.
One forward pass per token, no bookkeeping — latency scales linearly with output length and nowhere else. This makes it ideal for latency-critical systems.

By exploring k candidate sequences simultaneously, beam search regularly finds outputs that greedy decoding misses, especially in structured tasks like translation, summarization, and code generation where early token choices have high downstream impact.

Stochastic decoding lets the model explore lower-probability continuations, producing varied outputs across runs. This is essential for brainstorming, creative writing, data augmentation, and any use case where uniqueness matters.
Temperature scaling provides a single dial for creativity vs. precision.
Adjusting temperature reshapes the entire probability distribution without retraining the model, offering fine-grained control over output randomness at zero additional cost.

By restricting sampling to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p, nucleus sampling adaptively eliminates the long tail of very-low-probability (often semantically wrong) tokens while still allowing variety in the high-probability region.
Decoding is model-agnostic and zero-cost to change.
Switching decoding strategies requires no retraining — only an inference-time configuration change. This makes it cheap to experiment and iterate.
Greedy decoding is locally optimal but globally suboptimal.

Picking the highest-probability token at each step is a hill-climbing heuristic. It can lead to degenerate outputs (repetitive loops) and misses sequences where a slightly less probable early token enables a much better completion.
Beam search is k× slower per step and consumes k× more memory.

Each beam is an independent partial sequence that the model must forward-pass at each decoding step. With beam width k=5 and output length 512 tokens, the model runs 2,560 forward passes instead of 512. KV-cache memory also scales with k.
Beam search can produce generic, conservative outputs.
The globally highest-probability sequence tends toward common, safe text. For creative tasks or tasks requiring unusual outputs, beam search often underperforms sampling approaches.
Reproducibility requires fixing the random seed, and even then small numerical differences across GPU/CPU and batch orderings can affect outputs. This complicates debugging and evaluation.

As temperature increases toward 1 or beyond, low-probability tokens become more likely. Above temperature ≈ 1.2, outputs often become incoherent or semantically wrong because the model starts treating implausible tokens as near-equally likely to plausible ones.
Beam search and sampling do not compose simply.
Combining beam search with sampling (e.g., diverse beam search, stochastic beam search) requires careful hyperparameter tuning. Naive composition often gives the downsides of both methods.
, greedy decoding selects the token with the highest probability:
at each step. The complete greedy decoding algorithm runs in
is the number of generated tokens and
is the vocabulary size (typically 32k–128k). In practice the argmax is dominated by the model's forward pass cost, not the argmax computation itself.
candidate sequences (beams) at each step. At timestep
of these by cumulative log-probability are retained as the new beams:

Log-probabilities are used (rather than raw probabilities) because they convert products into sums, preventing numerical underflow over long sequences and simplifying comparison. The search terminates when all beams have emitted an EOS token or when the maximum length is reached. The beam with the highest score is returned as the output. Length normalization is often applied to avoid favoring shorter sequences:
is a length penalty exponent (commonly 0.6–0.9). Higher
Temperature scaling reshapes the probability distribution before sampling. Given logits
, the distribution sharpens (high-probability tokens become even more dominant). When
, the distribution flattens (more equal probability across tokens).
approaches greedy decoding. A token is then sampled from this adjusted distribution.
Top-k sampling truncates the distribution to the
most probable tokens, renormalizes their probabilities, and then samples:
is the normalizing constant. A fixed
is a crude approximation: in flat distributions many tokens share similar probability, and cutting at a fixed count may exclude meaningful options or include near-zero-probability noise.
Top-p sampling, introduced by Holtzman et al. (2020), selects the smallest set of tokens
such that their cumulative probability exceeds
. The tokens are sorted in descending probability order, and tokens are included until the sum reaches

Probabilities within the nucleus are renormalized and a token is drawn. The key advantage is that the effective vocabulary size adapts dynamically: when the model is confident (peaked distribution), the nucleus may contain just 1–5 tokens; when the model is uncertain (flat distribution), the nucleus might span hundreds of tokens. This adaptive truncation avoids both over-restriction and over-permissiveness, which fixed-k top-k cannot do.

The diagram below shows the shared pipeline all decoding methods share: an input context feeds the language model's forward pass to produce a vocabulary distribution, which is then consumed by the chosen decoding strategy to emit a token. The process repeats until a stopping criterion fires.

attrs.alt: Decoding Methods architecture — Greedy, Beam Search, and Sampling strategies consuming vocabulary distribution

content.text: takes the argmax at every step;
maintains k candidate sequences and scores them by cumulative log-probability; and

(temperature, top-k, top-p) reshape or truncate the distribution before drawing a random token. All three funnel into a generated token sequence that is appended to the context for the next step, and generation halts when a stopping criterion (EOS token, max token limit, or stop sequence) is met.
In beam search, the 'Language Model' box is invoked

times per decoding step (once per beam), making the forward-pass cost the primary bottleneck. Modern serving frameworks handle this by batching all k beams into a single forward pass with appropriately expanded KV-cache entries.
Latency is the primary constraint and minor quality trade-offs are acceptable.
The task is highly constrained and the model is confident (e.g., structured extraction, code completion where there is one right answer).
Deterministic reproducibility is required without managing random seeds.
The output will be short (greedy's suboptimality compounds over longer sequences).
The task has a well-defined correct output and quality is more important than speed: machine translation, summarization, structured prediction.
You need to generate multiple distinct candidate outputs (return top-k beams) for downstream ranking or selection.
You can afford k× inference cost and k× KV-cache memory.
The grammar or format constraints make the output space highly structured (beam search respects token-level constraints more easily than sampling).
Diversity across multiple runs is desired: creative writing, data augmentation, dialogue systems.
The model is large and well-calibrated, so the probability distribution is already reasonable — sampling exploits it rather than over-riding it.
You are using chain-of-thought prompting with self-consistency: sample N completions and take a majority vote over final answers.
The task benefits from exploration (agentic reasoning, hypothesis generation, tool selection when multiple tools could apply).
for long-form generation — repetition loops are common and quality degrades.
for creative or open-ended tasks — it produces generic, high-frequency text.
for factual or code generation — incoherence and hallucination rates increase sharply.
Most practitioners interact with decoding through library APIs (Hugging Face
, vLLM SamplingParams, OpenAI API parameters). The following implementation shows the mechanics from scratch to build intuition, then demonstrates the production API usage.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from typing import List, Optional

# ── Greedy decoding from raw logits ──────────────────────────────────────────

def greedy_decode(logits: torch.Tensor) -> int:
    """
    Args:
        logits: shape (vocab_size,) — raw unnormalised scores from the model
    Returns:
        Index of the selected token
    """
    return int(logits.argmax(dim=-1).item())


# ── Temperature + top-p sampling ─────────────────────────────────────────────

def sample_token(
    logits: torch.Tensor,
    temperature: float = 1.0,
    top_p: float = 1.0,
    top_k: int = 0,
) -> int:
    """
    Args:
        logits:      shape (vocab_size,)
        temperature: > 1 flattens distribution; < 1 sharpens it; 0 → greedy
        top_p:       nucleus sampling threshold (0, 1]; 1.0 disables
        top_k:       if > 0, restrict to top-k tokens before sampling
    Returns:
        Index of the sampled token
    """
    if temperature == 0.0:
        return greedy_decode(logits)

    # 1. Temperature scaling
    scaled = logits / temperature

    # 2. Top-k filtering
    if top_k > 0:
        top_k_values, _ = torch.topk(scaled, top_k)
        threshold = top_k_values[..., -1, None]
        scaled = scaled.masked_fill(scaled < threshold, float('-inf'))

    # 3. Nucleus (top-p) filtering
    if top_p < 1.0:
        probs = F.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)
        sorted_probs, sorted_idx = probs.sort(descending=True)
        cumulative = sorted_probs.cumsum(dim=-1)
        # Remove tokens once cumulative probability exceeds top_p
        mask = cumulative - sorted_probs > top_p
        sorted_probs[mask] = 0.0
        # Scatter back to original ordering
        probs.scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
        probs = probs / probs.sum()  # renormalise
    else:
        probs = F.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)

    # 4. Sample
    return int(torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1).item())
Simple Example: Comparing Greedy vs Sampling on a Toy Vocabulary

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Hypothetical logits for a 6-token vocabulary
# ["cat", "sat", "the", "on", "mat", "a"]
logits = torch.tensor([1.2, 3.5, 0.8, 2.1, 3.4, 0.5])

probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
print("Token probabilities:")
vocab = ["cat", "sat", "the", "on", "mat", "a"]
for word, p in zip(vocab, probs.tolist()):
    print(f"  {word:5s}: {p:.4f}")

# Greedy: always picks "sat" (index 1, prob ≈ 0.43)
greedy_token = vocab[logits.argmax().item()]
print(f"\nGreedy pick: {greedy_token}")

# Sampling at T=1.0 (three runs — different each time)
torch.manual_seed(42)
for i in range(3):
    idx = int(torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item())
    print(f"Sample run {i+1}: {vocab[idx]}")

# Sampling at T=0.3 (sharpened — almost always picks "sat")
scaled_probs = F.softmax(logits / 0.3, dim=-1)
torch.manual_seed(42)
for i in range(3):
    idx = int(torch.multinomial(scaled_probs, 1).item())
    print(f"T=0.3 run  {i+1}: {vocab[idx]}")
Production Example: Hugging Face generate() with Configurable Decoding

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model_name = "gpt2"  # replace with any causal LM
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
model.eval()

prompt = "The future of artificial intelligence is"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

# ── 1. Greedy decoding ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
greedy_out = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=40,
    do_sample=False,           # greedy (or beam if num_beams > 1)
)
print("Greedy:", tokenizer.decode(greedy_out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# ── 2. Beam search (width = 5) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
beam_out = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=40,
    num_beams=5,
    early_stopping=True,
    length_penalty=0.8,        # alpha in the length normalisation formula
    no_repeat_ngram_size=3,    # penalise trigram repetition
)
print("Beam:", tokenizer.decode(beam_out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# ── 3. Nucleus sampling (top-p) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
sample_out = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=40,
    do_sample=True,
    temperature=0.8,
    top_p=0.92,
    top_k=0,                   # disable fixed-k truncation
    repetition_penalty=1.1,
)
print("Sample:", tokenizer.decode(sample_out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# ── 4. Return multiple beam candidates ───────────────────────────────────────
multi_out = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=40,
    num_beams=5,
    num_return_sequences=3,    # return top 3 beams
    early_stopping=True,
)
for i, seq in enumerate(multi_out):
    print(f"Beam {i+1}:", tokenizer.decode(seq, skip_special_tokens=True))

Consider a production document summarization service that processes 50,000 articles per day. The system uses a fine-tuned T5-large model and serves requests through a Ray Serve deployment with 4 A10G GPUs.
The engineering team conducted offline evaluation and found that beam search with

achieved ROUGE-2 scores 4.1 points higher than greedy decoding on their test set, justifying the 4× throughput reduction. They serve beam search for high-priority requests (subscribers) and greedy for background batch jobs (free tier). For news summary generation — where generic, clear, factual text is desirable — beam search consistently outperforms sampling, which sometimes introduces hallucinated details.

# Illustrative pattern: tiered decoding for a summarization API
from transformers import pipeline
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SummarizationRequest:
    text: str
    priority: str  # "high" | "standard"
    max_length: int = 128

class SummarizationService:
    def __init__(self, model_name: str):
        self.pipe = pipeline("summarization", model=model_name, device=0)

    def summarize(self, req: SummarizationRequest) -> str:
        common_kwargs = dict(
            max_length=req.max_length,
            min_length=30,
            no_repeat_ngram_size=3,
        )
        if req.priority == "high":
            # Quality-optimised: beam search
            result = self.pipe(
                req.text,
                num_beams=4,
                length_penalty=0.7,
                early_stopping=True,
                **common_kwargs,
            )
        else:
            # Speed-optimised: greedy
            result = self.pipe(
                req.text,
                num_beams=1,
                do_sample=False,
                **common_kwargs,
            )
        return result[0]["summary_text"]

Greedy decoding frequently enters repetition loops in long-form generation. Because each token is chosen purely for its local probability, the model can lock into a pattern where a repeated phrase has the highest conditional probability given the recent context. Adding
helps, but these are heuristic patches, not solutions. For long-form tasks, switch to sampling or beam search.
Setting Temperature = 0 Instead of do_sample = False
in some frameworks causes division by zero or numerical instability when softmax is computed as
to request greedy decoding explicitly, rather than driving temperature to zero.
In Hugging Face Transformers, setting both

mode, not regular beam search. This is a distinct algorithm. Many developers inadvertently activate it without realizing that beam search scoring no longer applies. Be explicit: for standard beam search, set
Ignoring Length Normalisation in Beam Search

Without length normalisation, beam search prefers shorter sequences because log-probability sums are always negative, and shorter sequences accumulate fewer negative terms. The result is undesirably brief outputs. Always set
Using top_p and top_k Together Without Understanding Interaction

Top-k and top-p are applied sequentially: top-k first, then top-p over the surviving tokens. With small k (e.g. k=5) and p=0.9, top-p has no effect because the top-5 tokens often already have cumulative probability > 0.9. The result is effectively top-k sampling, not nucleus sampling. Either use one or the other, or understand the interaction before combining.
Start with a decoding method that matches the task objective.
Factual extraction and structured output → greedy or low-temperature sampling. Translation and summarization → beam search. Creative tasks → top-p sampling at T ∈ [0.7, 1.0].
Use top-p (nucleus) over top-k for sampling.
Top-p adapts to the model's confidence level at each step. Top-k does not — a fixed k will include too many tokens when the distribution is peaked and too few when it is flat.
Set repetition_penalty or no_repeat_ngram_size for any method.
Autoregressive models have a known tendency toward repetition. Penalising recently generated n-grams is a cheap and effective heuristic for all decoding strategies.
Apply length normalisation in beam search.
in the range [0.6, 1.0] for most tasks. Lower values produce more concise outputs; higher values allow longer sequences to compete.
Fix the random seed in experiments.
When evaluating sampling-based outputs, set a deterministic seed for reproducibility. In production, explicit non-determinism is usually fine, but evaluation requires reproducibility.
Use self-consistency sampling for reasoning tasks.

Sample N completions with temperature 0.7–1.0, extract the final answer from each, and take the majority vote. This reliably outperforms both greedy and single-sample decoding on benchmarks like GSM8K.
Profile decoding cost before committing to beam search.
Beam search requires k× more KV-cache memory and k× more model forward passes per step. For generation lengths above 200 tokens with large models, this can be prohibitive at production scale.
Decoding cost in autoregressive LLMs is dominated by the model's forward pass for each token, not the decoding algorithm itself. For a model with
per token (using KV-cache). The decoding algorithm adds:
per token for the argmax. Negligible relative to the forward pass.
per token for the top-k selection across all beam expansions, plus k× memory for KV-cache. Throughput scales roughly as 1/k vs. greedy.
for top-p (requires a sort). For typical vocab sizes this is fast in practice, especially with hardware-accelerated kernels.

At scale, the main levers for decoding throughput are: (1) continuous batching — process multiple requests' decoding steps in parallel, filling GPU cycles across requests at different decoding positions; (2) speculative decoding — use a small draft model to propose multiple tokens, then verify them with the large model in a single forward pass, achieving 2–3× throughput gains; and (3) structured output decoding — constrain the vocabulary to grammatically valid tokens only at each step, allowing greedy decoding to produce structured JSON/code without beam search.
is the number of attention layers and

is the head dimension. For a 7B parameter model with 32 layers and 128 head dimension, a single sequence with T=512 tokens occupies roughly 256 MB of KV-cache. Beam width k=5 multiplies this to 1.28 GB — a meaningful constraint on batch size.
method provides all major decoding strategies via
with temperature, top-k, top-p), diverse beam search (
), and constrained beam search via logits processors.
(analogous to OpenAI API parameters). Beam search is not natively supported in vLLM for throughput reasons — at high concurrency, continuous batching with sampling is more efficient.
The OpenAI chat completion API exposes
. The OpenAI guidance is to alter
but not both simultaneously (they interact). OpenAI does not expose beam search.
Libraries like Outlines and Microsoft Guidance implement

by masking invalid vocabulary tokens at each decoding step according to a grammar (JSON schema, regex, CFG). This enables greedy decoding to reliably produce valid structured outputs without any prompting tricks.

Decoding method directly influences hallucination rates. High-temperature sampling (T > 0.9) significantly increases the probability that low-confidence tokens (which often correspond to factually wrong text) are selected. For factual Q&A, code generation, and agentic tool-call generation, use low temperature (T ≤ 0.2) or greedy decoding to keep outputs close to the model's peak-probability (most-trained) behaviors.

Structured decoding libraries (Outlines, Guidance) apply logit masks derived from grammar definitions. If the grammar is user-controlled or partially user-influenced (e.g., "generate JSON matching schema provided by the user"), a malicious user can craft a grammar that forces the model to produce specific output — bypassing content policies. Always validate user-provided schemas/grammars server-side before using them to constrain decoding.
Repetition Loops and Denial of Service
Greedy decoding is vulnerable to repetition loops that fill the entire context window. Without

configured, a model in a greedy repetition loop will generate until it fills the maximum context length, consuming GPU cycles proportional to context length. Always set explicit generation length limits in serving infrastructure.

Agentic systems that make decisions (tool calls, branching logic) based on generated text must handle the non-determinism of sampling. A sampling-based agent that calls the same tool twice in two runs may call different tools. In safety-critical systems, use deterministic decoding or constrain sampling via structured output grammars and validate all tool calls independently of the text that triggered them.

Think of decoding as navigating a tree of possible continuations. At each node, the model assigns a probability to every branch (every vocabulary token). The tree has branching factor equal to the vocabulary size (~50,000) and depth equal to the output length.
always descends the single highest-probability branch at each level. It finds a good path quickly but has no backtracking — a mistaken early branch is permanent.

descends k branches simultaneously, pruning all but the top-k at each level by cumulative score. It approximates a broader tree search without exploding compute. The key insight is that even with k=5, beam search misses the globally optimal sequence — it is not exact search (the tree is too large to search exactly). But it reliably finds better paths than greedy by exploring multiple promising directions.

rolls a weighted die at each node. Temperature determines how lopsided the die is. Top-p removes the lowest-probability faces from the die before rolling. The result is that sampling traces a random path weighted toward the likely parts of the tree, without the deterministic tendency to always follow the same branches. Over multiple runs, sampling explores a diverse set of paths — which is exactly what you want when the goal is variety, not optimality.

A useful practical heuristic: if you can clearly state what the single correct output looks like, use greedy or beam search. If the space of good outputs is broad and any diverse, plausible completion is acceptable, use top-p sampling.
Decoding converts per-token probability distributions into actual sequences.
It is a separate, post-inference step that does not modify model weights but dramatically shapes output quality and character.
Greedy decoding is the fastest but globally suboptimal.
Use it for short, highly constrained outputs where the model is confident. Avoid it for long-form generation where repetition loops are common.
Beam search improves quality by exploring k sequences simultaneously.
It reliably outperforms greedy on translation, summarization, and structured generation at k× computational cost. Apply length normalisation to avoid bias toward short outputs.
Temperature controls the sharpness of sampling.
Values below 1 tighten toward greedy; values above 1 increase randomness. Do not use temperature > 1 for factual tasks.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling is the best general-purpose stochastic decoding method.
Its dynamic truncation adapts to the model's confidence at each step, unlike fixed top-k.
Decoding strategy affects hallucination and reliability.

High temperature and unconstrained sampling increase the probability of factually wrong tokens being chosen. In production agentic systems, use deterministic or low-temperature decoding for tool calls and structured reasoning.
Self-consistency sampling outperforms single-pass greedy on reasoning benchmarks.
Sample N completions at moderate temperature and take the majority answer — this is a practical way to improve accuracy without changing the model.
Unlike fine-tuning or prompting, switching between decoding strategies is an inference-time configuration change with zero training cost. Experimentation is nearly free.
